title: Comments & Documentation
description: Learn to write notes in your code that Python ignores.

slides.title: Talking to Yourself (and Others) 💬
slides.content:
# What Are Comments?

**Comments** are notes you write in your code that Python completely ignores!

They're for:

* 📝 Explaining what your code does
* 🧠 Reminding yourself why you wrote something
* 👥 Helping others understand your code

Think of comments as sticky notes for your code!

quizData.question: What happens when Python sees a comment?

options.text: It runs the comment as code

quizData.explanation: Python completely ignores comments — they're just for humans to read!

slides.title: Single-Line Comments #️⃣
slides.content:
# The Hash Symbol

Use the `#` symbol to create a comment:

```python
# This is a comment
print("Hello")  # This is also a comment
```

Everything after `#` on that line is ignored by Python!

slides.title: Comments in Action 💻
slides.content: See how Python ignores the comments but runs the code:
slides.codeSnippet:
# This line is completely ignored
print("This runs!")  # comment after code

# The next line greets the user
print("Hello, coder!")

quizData.question: Which symbol starts a comment in Python?
quizData.explanation: In Python, the # symbol starts a comment. Other languages use different symbols!

slides.title: Where to Place Comments 📍
slides.content:
# Comment Placement

You can place comments:

**1. On their own line:**
```python
# Calculate the total
total = price + tax
```

**2. After code (inline):**
```python
total = price + tax  # Add tax to price
```

Both are valid and useful!

quizData.question: Which is a valid inline comment?
quizData.explanation: Inline comments go after the code on the same line, starting with #

slides.title: Multi-Line Comments 📃
slides.content:
# Longer Explanations

For multiple lines, you have two options:

**Option 1: Multiple # symbols**
```python
# This is line one
# This is line two
# This is line three
```

**Option 2: Triple quotes (docstring)**
```python
"""
This is a
multi-line
comment
"""
```

slides.title: Multi-Line Examples 💻
slides.content: Both styles work for longer comments:
slides.codeSnippet:
# This program demonstrates
# how to use multiple
# comment lines

"""
This is another way to write
a longer explanation using
triple quotes!
"""

print("Comments help explain code!")

quizData.question: Which can create a multi-line comment?

options.text: Both # on each line and triple quotes

quizData.explanation: You can use multiple # symbols or triple quotes (""") for multi-line comments!

slides.title: Why Comment? 🤔
slides.content:
# The Importance of Comments

**You WILL forget** what your code does! Even experts forget.

Good comments:

* 🔍 Explain **why**, not just what
* 📖 Make code readable months later
* 🤝 Help teammates understand your thinking
* 🐛 Help you debug problems faster

slides.title: Good vs Bad Comments 📝
slides.content:
# Writing Helpful Comments

**❌ Bad comment (states the obvious):**
```python
x = 5  # Set x to 5
```

**✅ Good comment (explains why):**
```python
x = 5  # Default retry attempts for API calls
```

Explain the **purpose**, not the syntax!

quizData.question: Which is a better comment for: age = 18?
quizData.explanation: Good comments explain WHY — 'Minimum voting age requirement' explains the purpose!

slides.title: Comment Warnings ⚠️
slides.content:
## Avoid These Mistakes!

* **Too many comments** — Don't comment every single line
* **Outdated comments** — Update comments when you change code!
* **Obvious comments** — `x = x + 1  # Add 1 to x` (we can see that!)
* **No comments at all** — Complex code NEEDS explanation

Balance is key! 🎯

slides.title: Real-World Example 💻
slides.content: Here's how comments might look in a real program:
slides.codeSnippet:
# Calculate the final price with discount
price = 100
discount = 0.2  # 20% holiday sale

# Apply discount only if price exceeds minimum
final_price = price * (1 - discount)

print("Final price:", final_price)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Comment as You Code!

Don't wait until the end to add comments. Write them **as you code**:

1. ✍️ Write a comment explaining what you're about to do
2. 💻 Then write the code
3. 🔄 Update the comment if needed

This helps you think through your solution AND documents it at the same time!

quizData.question: What will this code output? # print("Hello")
quizData.explanation: The entire line is a comment because it starts with #, so nothing is printed!
